main_summary.summary: Dion Diamond, interviewed by David Cline on December 13, 2015, recounts his trajectory from Petersburg, Virginia, where he conducted spontaneous solo sit-ins at five-and-dime lunch counters as a teenager, through Howard University's Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), the Freedom Rides, SNCC field organizing in Mississippi and Louisiana, and post-Movement careers at the U.S. Department of Labor, in economic-development training, and at HUD and HEW. Diamond entered Howard in September 1959 as a physics major. After the Greensboro sit-ins, NAG desegregated the Drug Fair lunch counter in Arlington, Virginia, within two weeks and picketed Glen Echo Park through the summer of 1960. He joined the Freedom Rides after the Anniston bus burning, was arrested in Jackson on a breach-of-peace charge, and moved through the Hinds County jail, prison farm, and Parchman Farm, where he was cellmate to James Farmer. Reassigned to SNCC voter-registration work in Pike County, Mississippi, around McComb, he shared a house with Robert "Bob" Parris Moses and survived a shotgun blast through the bedroom window. Later arrested in Baton Rouge on a criminal-anarchy charge for organizing a Southern University boycott, he served solitary at the East Baton Rouge Parish jail. He transferred to the University of Wisconsin in September 1963, later attended Harvard's graduate school of education, and worked in housing, anti-poverty, and Medicaid/Medicare fraud-detection consulting.
key_themes: Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) at Howard University
Freedom Rides and Parchman Farm incarceration
SNCC voter registration in Pike County, Mississippi
Nonviolence as tactic rather than philosophy
main_summary.historical_significance:
Diamond's testimony documents the Washington, D.C. movement infrastructure that operated parallel to better-known Nashville and Atlanta organizing, specifically the Nonviolent Action Group at Howard, which desegregated Arlington's Drug Fair counters within two weeks of Greensboro and sustained the Glen Echo Park picket line through the summer of 1960. His firsthand account of the cell-by-cell movement of Freedom Riders from the Hinds County jail through the prison farm to Parchman provides primary-source detail on the scale of the arrests, including his recollection that he shared a Parchman cell with CORE director James Farmer and later a McComb safe house with Robert "Bob" Parris Moses.

Diamond's articulation of nonviolence as tactic rather than philosophy or religion, grounded in the practical observation that "the police and the army have the guns", is a counter-voice to the canonical Lawson/King framing and reflects the diversity of theoretical positions within SNCC. His account of the Robert F. Kennedy–Mississippi governor agreement to arrest rather than permit beatings of Riders, and of Cold War media pressure as the operative leverage, contributes to the historiography of federal-state negotiation during the Rides.

chapters.title: Petersburg and First Sit-Ins
chapters.summary: Diamond describes growing up in segregated Petersburg, Virginia, attending segregated schools, and conducting spontaneous solo sit-ins at five-and-dime lunch counters and the local library's colored entrance before sit-ins had a name. He locates the source of his resistance as instinct rather than influence, since Petersburg offered no integrated activity to model.

chapters.title: Monticello and First Integrated Contact
chapters.summary: At sixteen, Diamond traveled to Monticello, New York to work in the Catskills resort-laundry economy. Unable to find laundry work, he talked his way into a short-order cook job at the Frontier Restaurant by fabricating references through his aunt Janet Morgan in New Brunswick. This was his first sustained contact with white and Jewish communities outside the South.

chapters.title: Howard NAG and Arlington Drug Fair
chapters.summary: Diamond entered Howard University in September 1959 as a physics major. After the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, students at Howard organized the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) and crossed the Potomac to conduct their first sit-in at the Drug Fair lunch counter in Cherrydale, Arlington, Virginia. The chain was desegregated within two weeks, during which George Lincoln Rockwell and the American Nazi Party surrounded the demonstrators.

chapters.title: Nonviolence as Tactic
chapters.summary: Diamond articulates his view that nonviolence is a tactic, not a philosophy or religious path, grounded in the practical assessment that the police and army held the firepower. He contrasts his position with co-presenters who hold nonviolence as a way of life or religious commitment, and acknowledges that off-camera and outside sit-ins he would not turn the other cheek.

chapters.title: Glen Echo Park Picket Line
chapters.summary: NAG turned next to Glen Echo Park, a Maryland amusement park that bused in white children from the city but barred Black patrons. Unable to sit-in inside the park, the group picketed its perimeter through the summer of 1960. Diamond describes Hyman Bookbinder, then a labor-movement figure, arranging a black limousine, white chauffeur, and white interpreter so Diamond could approach the entrance in African dress, attempting in mock-pidgin to gain admission.

chapters.title: Freedom Rides to Jackson
chapters.summary: After the Anniston, Alabama bus burning nearly ended the Freedom Rides, Diamond and others traveled to Montgomery to continue the ride from Montgomery to Jackson, Mississippi. He describes the National Guard escort transfer at the Alabama-Mississippi state line, the immediate arrest upon stepping off the bus toward the white waiting room, and the breach-of-peace charge. He links the arrest-rather-than-beat outcome to a closed-door agreement between the Mississippi governor and Robert F. Kennedy during Cold War tensions with Khrushchev.

chapters.title: Hinds County Jail to Parchman
chapters.summary: Arrested at the Jackson bus terminal, Diamond was held in the Hinds County jail, then moved to the county prison farm, then to the state penitentiary at Parchman Farm as later busloads of Riders filled the system. Segregated by race and sex inside the jails, he shared a Parchman cell with CORE director James Farmer. Diamond recalls being humbled by Farmer's monosyllabic reserve in conversation.
related_events: Mass Freedom Rider arrests Jackson Mississippi 1961

chapters.title: McComb and the Shotgun Blast
chapters.summary: After Parchman, Diamond flew to Atlanta and accepted a SNCC assignment in Pike County, Mississippi, around McComb. He shared a house with Robert "Bob" Parris Moses, hosted by a woman the organizers called Mama Cotton. One night a shotgun blast came through the bedroom lattice window and passed over Diamond's body, he notes that his frame was thin enough that the shot cleared him. The incident concretized the danger of Mississippi voter-registration work for him.

chapters.title: Southern University and Criminal Anarchy
chapters.summary: Sent to Holly Springs, Mississippi for voter registration, Diamond detoured to Baton Rouge after Southern University president Felton G. Clark closed the school and expelled student-protest leaders. Organizing a class boycott on a campus where he was a stranger, Diamond was arrested by state and campus police and charged with criminal anarchy, attempting to overthrow the state of Louisiana, along with trespassing and disorderly conduct misdemeanors. Placed initially in the East Baton Rouge Parish general jail population with convicted murderers, then moved to solitary confinement after FBI contact via an NAACP lawyer.
related_events: Southern University Baton Rouge student protests 1961-62

chapters.title: Wisconsin, Harvard, and Career
chapters.summary: At a 1963 National Student Association Congress in Columbus, Ohio, Diamond was recruited on the spot to the University of Wisconsin by the student-government president, transferring from Howard and changing his major from physics to history and sociology to investigate why the emancipation-to-protest interval took a century. In 1966 he came to D.C. to work at the United Planning Organization; in 1968 he was offered three options, Marshall Islands anti-poverty administration, the Robert F. Kennedy campaign, or Harvard. RFK was assassinated; Diamond went to Harvard for graduate study in education with a thesis on race and ethnic relations. His career followed at the Department of Labor (GS-14), economic-development training, HUD, and HEW Medicaid/Medicare fraud-detection consulting.

chapters.title: Reflections on Progress and Loss
chapters.summary: Diamond reflects on the bittersweet quality of desegregation, the loss of close teacher-student-parent relationships in small segregated towns, the depletion of HBCU athletic programs as Big Ten schools recruited Black athletes, and the persistence of a glass ceiling beneath token CEO appointments. He critiques Black Lives Matter framing as segmenting an issue he sees as universal, names the Charleston church shooting as evidence the country has not cured the problem, and warns that what is past is prologue when discussing the Trump rallies he was watching at the time of the interview.

chapters.title: Civil Rights Act and Obama
chapters.summary: Asked for his most memorable moment, Diamond names the 1964 Civil Rights Act and the election of Barack Obama as the dual highlights of his lifetime, noting that neither he nor his SNCC peers in McComb in 1961 imagined Black elected officials beyond a single commissioner. He observes that one expected John F. Kennedy to act on civil rights but never Lyndon Johnson, a Southerner, to implement it.

loc_video.caption: Dion Diamond oral history interview conducted by David Cline in Washington, District of Columbia, 2015 December 13
